h1Title: Amaurosis Fugax: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Amaurosis fugax is a transient, monocular vision loss lasting seconds to minutes, caused by brief ischaemia of the retina or optic nerve, most commonly from an embolus arising in the ipsilateral internal carotid artery. The episode is typically painless and described by patients as a grey curtain descending over one eye. Amaurosis fugax constitutes a transient ischaemic attack (TIA) of the eye and carries the same short-term stroke risk as a hemispheric TIA — approximately 10–15% stroke risk within 90 days, with half of those strokes occurring within the first 48 hours. Patients must be assessed urgently as a TIA emergency: same-day or next-day vascular workup, aspirin, and carotid endarterectomy when a significant ipsilateral carotid stenosis is found. Early intervention reduces 5-year stroke risk by approximately 50%.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 1–8 per 100,000 per year in the general population; accounts for approximately 10% of all TIA presentations in neurovascular clinics
keyStats.demographics: Peak incidence in patients aged 50–75; men affected approximately 2× more than women; white populations at higher risk than black populations in most studies
keyStats.avgAge: Mean presentation age 60–70 years; rare before age 40 unless cardiac embolism, vasculitis, or antiphospholipid syndrome is present
keyStats.globalCases: Insufficient global incidence data; in the UK approximately 10,000–20,000 episodes of transient monocular vision loss are seen in TIA clinics annually

definition: Amaurosis fugax (ICD-10: G45.3), derived from the Greek 'amauros' (dark, dim) and Latin 'fugax' (fleeting), describes transient monocular blindness or marked visual dimming in one eye, lasting typically 1–15 minutes and resolving completely. It is classified as a retinal TIA. The most common underlying mechanism is atherothromboembolism from the ipsilateral internal carotid artery: atherosclerotic plaque at the carotid bifurcation or ICA origin generates fibrinoplatelet or cholesterol emboli that lodge in the central retinal artery or its branches, temporarily obstructing blood flow. Less common causes include cardioembolism (atrial fibrillation, valvular disease, patent foramen ovale), haematological conditions (polycythaemia, thrombocytosis), arteritis (giant cell arteritis in patients over 50), and primary ocular causes such as raised intraocular pressure or optic nerve head drusen. The condition is managed at the intersection of neurovascular medicine and ophthalmology. Identification of the underlying mechanism — particularly high-grade ipsilateral carotid stenosis — is critical because surgical or endovascular carotid revascularisation dramatically reduces subsequent stroke risk.

typesClassification.description: Atherothromboembolism from the ipsilateral internal carotid artery accounts for approximately 40–50% of amaurosis fugax. Risk of subsequent hemisphere stroke is directly proportional to the degree of carotid stenosis: 15% 5-year stroke risk with stenosis below 50%, rising to 25–30% with stenosis above 70% if untreated.

Emboli from atrial fibrillation, valvular disease, recent myocardial infarction, or intracardiac thrombus account for approximately 15–25% of cases. Cardioembolism should be suspected when bilateral ocular TIAs occur, when no carotid lesion is found, or when the patient has known cardiac disease.

Severe carotid stenosis (near-occlusion, string sign) or hypotension reduces ipsilateral retinal perfusion without discrete embolus. Vision loss is typically positional or altitude-related (worsens when standing, relieved by lying down). This mechanism accounts for approximately 5–10% of amaurosis fugax.

Retinal artery spasm can cause transient vision loss in young adults, migraine (retinal migraine), cocaine use, and Raynaud phenomena. The positive visual phenomena (scintillations, coloured lights) that often precede or accompany visual loss in migraine help differentiate this from embolic amaurosis fugax.

Raised intraocular pressure (acute angle-closure glaucoma or intermittent angle closure), optic disc drusen, and papilloedema can all produce transient monocular vision loss. These are excluded by slit-lamp and fundoscopic examination. Giant cell arteritis causing anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy can also produce transient vision loss before permanent visual loss occurs.

primarySymptoms: Sudden, painless, complete or near-complete loss of vision in one eye, typically developing over 1–5 seconds and lasting 1–15 minutes before full recovery — the classic presentation of embolic amaurosis fugax.

A grey, white, or black curtain, shade, or shutter descending vertically from above (most commonly) or ascending from below across the visual field of one eye — described in approximately 30–40% of classical carotid-embolic episodes.
Visual dimming or 'greying out' of one eye, as if looking through frosted glass, that brightens and recovers over 5–10 minutes.
In haemodynamic low-flow episodes, transient vision loss on standing from upright (postural) hypotension, or when entering bright light, resolving when the precipitant resolves.

Painless unless arteritic: giant cell arteritis causes amaurosis fugax accompanied by scalp tenderness, jaw claudication, temporal headache, and systemic symptoms — a distinct and urgent presentation.
No pain or other eye symptoms in the typical embolic form, which differentiates it from acute angle-closure glaucoma (severe eye pain and red eye) and optic neuritis (pain on eye movement).
Occasionally, awareness of bright light flashes or transient positive visual phenomena (blobs, sparkles) during recovery phase, reflecting reperfusion of the ischaemic retina.
Faint or tunnel vision that returns to complete normal function within minutes — a recovery pattern that distinguishes transient ischaemia from permanent retinal artery occlusion.

In severe embolic episodes, a Hollenhorst plaque (bright orange-yellow cholesterol crystal) may be visible at an arterial bifurcation on fundoscopy after the episode, confirming atherothromboembolism.
earlyWarningSigns: A first episode of transient monocular vision loss in a patient over 50 with vascular risk factors — this is a vascular emergency requiring same-day TIA clinic evaluation, not watchful waiting.
Ipsilateral carotid bruit detected incidentally on auscultation in a patient with any prior ocular TIA symptoms — triggers urgent carotid duplex ultrasound.

Hollenhorst plaque visible on fundoscopy (bright yellow refractile crystal at a retinal arteriolar bifurcation) in a patient reporting intermittent one-eye visual disturbances — indicates upstream carotid atheroma.
New onset of contralateral limb or facial TIA symptoms in a patient with previously treated amaurosis fugax — suggests inadequate secondary prevention or progression of carotid disease.
emergencySigns: Persistent vision loss beyond 60 minutes — this is no longer amaurosis fugax but central or branch retinal artery occlusion, a stroke equivalent requiring same-day ophthalmology and neurovascular assessment for reperfusion therapy.

Amaurosis fugax with simultaneous contralateral limb weakness, dysarthria, or facial droop — suggests carotid territory TIA involving both the retinal and hemisphere circulation; call emergency services immediately.

Temporal headache, scalp tenderness, jaw pain, and fever accompanying transient visual loss in any patient over 50 — suspect giant cell arteritis, where permanent blindness can develop within hours without immediate IV corticosteroids.
Loss of vision after a history of antecedent headache, diplopia, or ataxia — exclude posterior circulation pathology and central venous sinus thrombosis with urgent MRI.
Amaurosis fugax in a patient taking anticoagulants with very low or supratherapeutic INR — suggests sub- or supratherapeutic anticoagulation needing immediate drug level assessment.

causes.description: Atherosclerotic plaque at the carotid bifurcation or ICA origin generates fibrinoplatelet or cholesterol emboli that travel via the ophthalmic artery to the central retinal artery or its branches. The degree of stenosis predicts recurrence: high-grade stenosis (above 70%) carries a 5-year ipsilateral stroke risk of 26% without treatment. This is the single most important mechanistic finding because carotid endarterectomy or stenting dramatically reduces risk.

Atrial fibrillation is the most common cardiac source — AF increases thromboembolic risk 5-fold and accounts for approximately 15–25% of amaurosis fugax without ipsilateral carotid disease. Other cardiac sources: left ventricular thrombus post-MI, mitral stenosis, prosthetic heart valves, atrial myxoma, and paradoxical embolism through a patent foramen ovale (PFO). Cardiac causes are suspected when no carotid disease is found on duplex ultrasound.

Granulomatous vasculitis of medium and large vessels affecting predominantly adults over 50, with female predominance (3:1 ratio). Ischaemia of the ophthalmic artery or its branches causes amaurosis fugax as a warning before permanent anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy, which causes irreversible monocular blindness. ESR above 50 mm/hr and temporal artery tenderness are key clues; a temporal artery biopsy confirms the diagnosis.

Polycythaemia vera, thrombocythaemia, hyperviscosity syndromes, antiphospholipid syndrome, and severe anaemia can all cause transient retinal ischaemia from sluggish or turbulent blood flow. Antiphospholipid syndrome is particularly important in young adults presenting with amaurosis fugax without conventional vascular risk factors, as it requires long-term anticoagulation.

In young adults without vascular risk factors, transient monocular visual loss associated with migrainous symptoms — positive visual phenomena, subsequent unilateral headache, or scintillating scotoma — points to retinal artery vasospasm rather than embolism. The International Headache Society diagnostic criteria require fully reversible visual loss confirmed as monocular and associated with a migraine attack.

When carotid stenosis approaches 99%, flow distal to the stenosis falls so low that any physiological hypotension (standing, exertion, warm bath) can precipitate ischaemia in the retina and hemisphere. Low-flow amaurosis fugax tends to be longer in duration and positional, and occurs without discrete embolic symptoms.

riskFactors.description: The strongest modifiable risk factor; stenosis above 70% carries a 5-year stroke risk of 26% without intervention. Carotid atheroma is directly related to smoking, hypertension, hyperlipidaemia, and diabetes.

Systolic blood pressure above 140 mmHg is associated with a 3–4× increase in ischaemic stroke and TIA risk. Effective antihypertensive therapy reduces amaurosis fugax recurrence and subsequent stroke.

Diabetes doubles the risk of ischaemic stroke and accelerates carotid atherosclerosis through endothelial dysfunction and dyslipidaemia. Each 1% increase in HbA1c raises stroke risk by approximately 20%.
AF produces a 5-fold increase in cardioembolism. Anticoagulation with a DOAC reduces AF-related stroke by 60–70%. AF may be paroxysmal and require prolonged cardiac monitoring to detect.
Current smoking doubles the risk of ischaemic stroke and is one of the most potent modifiable accelerants of carotid atherosclerosis. Smoking cessation reduces stroke risk by 50% within 2–4 years.
LDL-cholesterol above 3.4 mmol/L (130 mg/dL) promotes atherosclerotic plaque formation at the carotid bifurcation. High-intensity statin therapy reduces carotid plaque vulnerability and stroke risk.
Atherosclerotic vascular disease accelerates with age; men have approximately 2× higher risk of ischaemic stroke than women under age 75. After the menopause, the sex gap narrows substantially.

affectedDemographics: Men over 60 are the predominant demographic, reflecting higher rates of carotid atherosclerosis in older males.
Patients with established cardiovascular disease — coronary artery disease, peripheral arterial disease — are at substantially elevated risk due to shared atherosclerotic mechanisms.
Young adults (under 40) presenting with amaurosis fugax are more likely to have a cardioembolic source, retinal migraine, or a thrombophilia — warrants a different diagnostic pathway.
Women over 50 presenting with amaurosis fugax should be specifically assessed for giant cell arteritis, which affects women 3× more than men and increases dramatically in incidence after age 70.
Patients of South Asian and East Asian ethnicity have different atherosclerotic plaque morphology and higher intracranial versus extracranial stenosis rates, which may alter management strategies.
diagnosisOverview: Every episode of transient monocular vision loss should be treated as a vascular emergency. The priority is to establish the likely mechanism and quantify stroke risk within the first 24–48 hours. Assessment begins with a structured TIA history: which eye, how long, associated symptoms (contralateral limb, speech, headache, jaw pain), vascular risk factors, and current medications. The ABCD2 score estimates short-term stroke risk but was developed for hemispheric TIA and has modest validation for ocular TIA. Carotid duplex ultrasound is the mandatory first vascular investigation: it measures the degree of ipsilateral ICA stenosis, plaque morphology, and velocity profiles. If duplex suggests significant stenosis (above 50% by NASCET criteria), CT angiography or MRI angiography of the carotid arteries is performed to confirm and plan revascularisation. Cardiac investigations include 12-lead ECG (to detect AF or recent MI), echocardiogram (for cardiac sources and PFO), and prolonged cardiac monitoring if paroxysmal AF is suspected. Retinal examination is essential: an ophthalmologist looks for Hollenhorst plaques, emboli at arterial bifurcations, signs of central or branch retinal artery occlusion, optic disc oedema (suggesting optic neuropathy), and raised intraocular pressure. Blood tests include FBC, ESR and CRP (elevated in giant cell arteritis — ESR above 50 mm/hr with unilateral temporal headache mandates immediate IV methylprednisolone), glucose, HbA1c, lipid profile, coagulation screen, and antiphospholipid antibodies in young patients. MRI brain with DWI detects coincident ischaemic lesions in the ipsilateral hemisphere, confirming embolic mechanism.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures degree of ipsilateral ICA stenosis, plaque characteristics (echogenicity, surface irregularity), and haemodynamic significance; guides revascularisation decision
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Non-invasive ultrasound probe applied to the neck. Takes 20–30 minutes. No radiation. Provides stenosis percentage (NASCET or ECST criteria), peak systolic velocity, and plaque morphology. Results available immediately or same-day.

diagnosticTests.test: CT angiography or MR angiography of carotid and cerebral vessels
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides precise stenosis measurement, identifies carotid dissection, and assesses intracranial vessels when carotid duplex shows stenosis above 50%
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: CT angiography requires an iodinated contrast injection via IV cannula; the scan takes 10–15 minutes. MRA avoids radiation and iodinated contrast (gadolinium used or non-contrast TOF technique). Images reconstructed to 3D for surgical planning.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects AF, cardiac rhythm disturbances, and recent MI as cardioembolic sources; 24–72 hour Holter monitor or implantable loop recorder for paroxysmal AF
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard ECG takes 5 minutes, placing electrodes on the chest and limbs. Holter monitoring involves wearing a small box with electrode leads for 1–3 days during normal activities. Results interpreted by a cardiologist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies intracardiac thrombus, valvular disease, wall-motion abnormalities from MI, atrial myxoma, and patent foramen ovale
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Transthoracic echo: ultrasound probe placed on the chest, 30–45 minutes, painless. Transoesophageal echo (TOE) requires swallowing a probe — performed under light sedation, superior sensitivity for left atrial thrombus and PFO.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies Hollenhorst plaques, central or branch retinal artery occlusions, disc oedema, and rules out primary ocular causes of vision loss
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Slit-lamp fundoscopy takes 10–15 minutes. Fluorescein angiography involves an IV dye injection with sequential retinal photos showing blood flow dynamics. Mild nausea from dye in approximately 5% of patients.

diagnosticTests.test: ESR, CRP, and temporal artery biopsy
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for giant cell arteritis in patients over 50 — the only immediately treatable cause of amaurosis fugax before permanent blindness occurs
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: ESR and CRP from a blood draw. Temporal artery biopsy (3 cm segment removed under local anaesthetic from the scalp, 20 minutes) confirms granulomatous vasculitis histologically. Steroids should not be withheld pending biopsy.

treatmentOverview: Management of amaurosis fugax follows the TIA emergency pathway. Antiplatelet therapy with aspirin 300 mg loading dose, then 75 mg daily, is started immediately and reduces subsequent stroke risk by approximately 15–20%. Clopidogrel 75 mg daily (or dual antiplatelet with aspirin plus clopidogrel for 21 days, per AHA/ASA guidelines 2021) is the preferred long-term strategy after carotid territory TIA in patients without AF. For patients with AF as the embolic source, anticoagulation with a direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC — apixaban, rivaroxaban, or dabigatran) reduces stroke risk by 60–70% and is strongly preferred over aspirin. High-intensity statin therapy (atorvastatin 40–80 mg daily) reduces LDL-cholesterol and carotid plaque vulnerability, lowering subsequent stroke risk by approximately 22% (SPARCL trial). Carotid endarterectomy (CEA) is the treatment of choice for symptomatic carotid stenosis of 70–99% (NASCET criteria), reducing 5-year ipsilateral stroke risk from 26% to 9% when performed by an experienced surgeon with perioperative mortality below 3%. CEA should be performed within 2 weeks of the amaurosis fugax episode for maximum benefit. Carotid artery stenting (CAS) is an alternative for patients who are not surgical candidates, though CAS has slightly higher peri-procedural stroke risk than CEA in older patients. Blood pressure control to below 130/80 mmHg, diabetes management, and smoking cessation complete secondary prevention. Giant cell arteritis requires immediate high-dose oral prednisolone (60 mg daily) or IV methylprednisolone (1 g daily for 3 days) to prevent permanent bilateral visual loss.

medicalTreatments.name: Aspirin (300 mg loading, then 75 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Antiplatelet agent that inhibits platelet cyclooxygenase and thromboxane A2. First-line antiplatelet for carotid-territory amaurosis fugax. Reduces subsequent stroke and vascular events by approximately 15–20%.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Relative risk reduction for stroke approximately 15% over placebo in TIA patients; absolute benefit greatest in high-risk patients (ABCD2 score ≥4)

medicalTreatments.name: Clopidogrel (75 mg daily) or dual antiplatelet (aspirin + clopidogrel for 21 days)
medicalTreatments.description: Clopidogrel monotherapy is preferred by most guidelines for long-term secondary prevention after TIA. Short-term dual antiplatelet therapy for 21 days (then clopidogrel alone or aspirin alone) reduces early recurrence after TIA — POINT and CHANCE trials demonstrate 25% relative reduction in stroke over 90 days.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: POINT trial: dual antiplatelet reduced 90-day stroke risk from 6.5% to 5.0% vs aspirin alone; NNT ≈ 67 over 90 days

medicalTreatments.name: Direct oral anticoagulant (DOAC) for AF-related amaurosis fugax (apixaban 5 mg twice daily, rivaroxaban 20 mg daily, or dabigatran 150 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: DOACs replace warfarin as first-line anticoagulation for non-valvular AF. They reduce AF-related stroke by 60–70% versus no anticoagulation and have lower major bleeding risk than warfarin.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: ARISTOTLE, ROCKET-AF, RE-LY trials: all DOACs reduced stroke by 60–70% vs. placebo; 10–20% lower major bleeding than warfarin

medicalTreatments.name: High-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40–80 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduces LDL-cholesterol and atherosclerotic plaque inflammation, lowering stroke risk after TIA. The SPARCL trial demonstrated a 22% relative reduction in stroke in patients with recent TIA or stroke treated with atorvastatin 80 mg.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: SPARCL trial: atorvastatin 80 mg reduced 5-year absolute stroke risk by 2.2 percentage points vs placebo (NNT ≈ 46)

medicalTreatments.name: Antihypertensive therapy (ACE inhibitor or ARB + thiazide diuretic)
medicalTreatments.description: Reduction of systolic BP from 145 to 130 mmHg reduces stroke risk by approximately 30–40%. Post-TIA BP targets are below 130/80 mmHg per AHA/ASA guidelines 2021. ACE inhibitors (ramipril 10 mg) and indapamide are the most evidence-based agents.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: PROGRESS trial: ramipril + indapamide reduced recurrent stroke by 43% versus placebo in TIA patients

medicalTreatments.name: High-dose prednisolone for giant cell arteritis (60 mg daily or IV methylprednisolone 1 g daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Immediate systemic corticosteroids prevent progression from transient to permanent monocular blindness in GCA. Must be started on clinical suspicion without waiting for biopsy result. Initiated by an ophthalmologist or rheumatologist.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Preserves vision in approximately 80–90% of patients when started before permanent ischaemic optic neuropathy develops

surgicalOptions.name: Carotid endarterectomy (CEA)
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of the atherosclerotic plaque from the internal carotid artery under general or locoregional anaesthesia. Reduces 5-year ipsilateral stroke risk from 26% to 9% for symptomatic stenosis 70–99% (NASCET trial). Should be performed within 2 weeks for maximum benefit.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year NNT ≈ 6 for stenosis 70–99%; absolute risk reduction 17 percentage points. Perioperative stroke or death rate must be below 3% per institutional standards

surgicalOptions.name: Carotid artery stenting (CAS)
surgicalOptions.description: Percutaneous endovascular placement of a stent in the stenotic ICA using distal embolic protection. Recommended as an alternative to CEA in patients with contralateral occlusion, post-radiation stenosis, or high surgical risk. CREST and ICSS trials show comparable long-term outcomes, though CAS has higher peri-procedural stroke risk in patients over 70.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term 5-year ipsilateral stroke risk equivalent to CEA in most trials; peri-procedural stroke risk 2–4% in experienced centres

alternativeTreatments.name: Acupuncture and lifestyle intervention
alternativeTreatments.description: No RCT evidence supports acupuncture for amaurosis fugax. Smoking cessation (halves stroke risk within 2–4 years), regular aerobic exercise (reduces stroke risk by 25–30%), and Mediterranean diet (reduces cardiovascular events by 30% in PREDIMED trial) are the evidence-based lifestyle interventions.

whySeeSpecialist: Amaurosis fugax must be assessed by a neurologist or stroke specialist within 24 hours as a TIA emergency. Urgent carotid duplex and cardiac evaluation directly determine whether carotid surgery or anticoagulation is needed, and this decision must be made within 2 weeks to maximise stroke risk reduction.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a TIA/stroke specialist with immediate access to carotid duplex, cardiac monitoring, and vascular surgery. The optimal assessment occurs at a rapid-access TIA clinic combining neurovascular medicine, vascular surgery, and ophthalmology. For giant cell arteritis, rheumatology or ophthalmology with experience in vasculitis is essential.
hospitalCriteria: 24-hour rapid-access TIA clinic with same-day carotid duplex ultrasound capability
Cardiac monitoring (12-lead ECG, Holter, echocardiography) available for same-day or next-day assessment
Ophthalmology service for retinal examination and exclusion of ocular causes
Vascular surgery service performing carotid endarterectomy within 2 weeks for high-grade stenosis
Interventional neuroradiology for carotid artery stenting in non-surgical candidates
keyFacilities: Neurovascular TIA clinic for urgent triage and risk stratification
Carotid duplex and CT angiography unit
Cardiology unit for Holter monitoring and echocardiography
preventionStrategies: Control blood pressure to below 130/80 mmHg — hypertension is the single most important modifiable vascular risk factor for amaurosis fugax and stroke.
Stop smoking — smoking cessation halves ischaemic stroke risk within 2–4 years and slows carotid atherosclerosis progression.
Treat AF with a DOAC to prevent cardioembolic amaurosis fugax and stroke.
Reduce LDL-cholesterol to below 2.0 mmol/L (77 mg/dL) in patients with established carotid atherosclerosis using high-intensity statin therapy.
Manage diabetes mellitus with target HbA1c below 53 mmol/mol (7%) to reduce cerebrovascular complications.
lifestyleModifications: Take antiplatelet or anticoagulant medication every day without interruption — missing doses dramatically increases the risk of stroke after TIA.
Monitor blood pressure at home twice daily and share readings with the treating team; target below 130/80 mmHg.
Attend all follow-up vascular clinic appointments — carotid disease progresses and repeat imaging may be needed.
Report any recurrent vision disturbance, limb weakness, or speech difficulty immediately as an emergency — do not wait until the next appointment.
Follow a Mediterranean-style diet rich in vegetables, olive oil, fish, and whole grains.
Perform at least 150 minutes per week of moderate aerobic exercise (walking, cycling) once medically cleared.

recommended: Mediterranean diet (olive oil, vegetables, legumes, fish, nuts) — PREDIMED trial showed 30% reduction in cardiovascular events
Oily fish (salmon, mackerel, sardines) two portions per week for omega-3 fatty acids
Low-sodium foods (below 5 g salt per day) to support blood pressure reduction
High-fibre foods (oats, beans, lentils, fruit) to lower LDL-cholesterol
avoid: High-sodium processed and fast foods that elevate blood pressure
Saturated and trans fats (red meat, butter, processed snacks) that raise LDL-cholesterol
Excessive alcohol (above 14 units per week raises blood pressure and AF risk)
Grapefruit juice with statins (inhibits CYP3A4 metabolism of atorvastatin)

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate aerobic exercise (30 minutes, five times per week) reduces stroke risk by approximately 25–30%. Walking is the safest and most accessible form. Heavy isometric exercise (weightlifting) that raises blood pressure acutely should be avoided until blood pressure is well-controlled. Exercise referral programmes can support patients who are deconditioned after a vascular event.
dailyManagement: Take antiplatelet (aspirin or clopidogrel) or anticoagulant at the same time each day with food.
Measure blood pressure each morning; document the reading and aim for systolic below 130 mmHg consistently.
Take statin medication every evening — statins are most effective when taken at night when hepatic cholesterol synthesis peaks.
Do not drive until cleared by the treating physician and local licensing authority — TIA and amaurosis fugax carry driving restrictions (typically 1 month in the UK).
Keep an episode diary: date, duration, which eye, associated symptoms, precipitating factors; share with the clinician at follow-up.
Know the warning signs for an approaching complete stroke — FAST (Face, Arms, Speech, Time to call emergency services) — and act within minutes.
prognosis: The prognosis of amaurosis fugax is dominated by the risk of subsequent ischaemic stroke rather than permanent visual loss. Without treatment, the 90-day stroke risk is approximately 10–15%, with 50% of those strokes occurring within the first 48 hours — the same risk profile as a hemispheric TIA. With optimised secondary prevention (antiplatelet, statin, blood pressure control), this 90-day risk falls to approximately 4–5%. Carotid endarterectomy for high-grade symptomatic stenosis (70–99%) reduces 5-year ipsilateral stroke risk from 26% to 9% (NNT ≈ 6). Visual prognosis is generally excellent when amaurosis fugax has fully resolved — permanent visual loss occurs in fewer than 1% of classic embolic episodes. However, giant cell arteritis is an exception: without treatment, 30–50% of patients proceed to permanent monocular or bilateral blindness within weeks. Long-term, patients with amaurosis fugax retain a 3–5× elevated risk of coronary artery disease, peripheral arterial disease, and stroke compared with age-matched controls without vascular disease.
recoveryTimeline: Vision returns to normal within 15–30 minutes of the episode in the vast majority of cases. Vascular workup is completed within 24–48 hours. Secondary preventive treatments (antiplatelet, statin, antihypertensive) are initiated within 24 hours. Carotid endarterectomy, when indicated, is performed within 2 weeks of the index event. Full secondary prevention effect accumulates over 3–6 months. Long-term annual vascular review is recommended indefinitely.
complications: Ischaemic stroke — the most serious complication; occurs in 10–15% of untreated patients within 90 days; half within 48 hours. Early vascular assessment and treatment are the most important interventions.

Central retinal artery occlusion — the permanent equivalent of amaurosis fugax; causes complete monocular blindness with cherry-red spot on fundoscopy; requires urgent ophthalmology management within 4–6 hours if reperfusion therapy is considered.
Permanent visual field loss from branch retinal artery occlusion — occurs in approximately 5% of embolic episodes that are incompletely cleared; presents as persistent scotoma.
Coronary artery disease — the leading cause of death in patients with carotid atherosclerosis and amaurosis fugax; annual cardiovascular review is recommended.
Contralateral carotid disease progression — atherosclerosis is a systemic process; the contralateral carotid artery requires regular duplex surveillance.
Carotid endarterectomy complications — perioperative stroke (1–3%), myocardial infarction (1–2%), cranial nerve injury (5–8% transient), and wound haematoma (2–5%) in specialist centres.

supportResources.name: Stroke Association (UK)
supportResources.description: Comprehensive patient information, helpline, and post-TIA support resources covering amaurosis fugax and stroke prevention.

supportResources.name: American Stroke Association — TIA Information
supportResources.description: Patient-facing TIA education from the American Heart Association and American Stroke Association including warning sign recognition and action plans.

supportResources.name: RNIB (Royal National Institute of Blind People)
supportResources.description: Support for patients experiencing visual loss from any cause, including emergency ophthalmology guidance and visual rehabilitation.

supportResources.name: NHS Inform — Amaurosis Fugax
supportResources.description: NHS Scotland evidence-based TIA patient guide covering recognition, emergency response, and secondary prevention.

confusedWithConditions.name: Stroke
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Amaurosis fugax fully resolves within 60 minutes and leaves no permanent deficit. Ischaemic stroke (retinal artery occlusion or cortical visual loss) is permanent or partially resolving. Brain imaging distinguishes the two: amaurosis fugax shows no cortical infarct on DWI MRI, while cortical stroke affecting vision produces a posterior circulation lesion involving the occipital cortex.

confusedWithConditions.name: Glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute angle-closure glaucoma causes transient or persistent monocular vision loss accompanied by severe eye pain, nausea, a red eye, and a fixed mid-dilated pupil. Amaurosis fugax is painless with a normal-appearing eye. Tonometry showing raised IOP (above 30 mmHg) confirms glaucoma; normal IOP and carotid bruit points to amaurosis fugax.

confusedWithConditions.name: Migraine
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Visual aura in migraine is typically binocular (involves both eyes, representing occipital cortex), moves across the visual field over 20–30 minutes, and is often described as shimmering or flickering lights (scintillating scotoma). Amaurosis fugax is strictly monocular, painless, and sudden. Retinal migraine is a distinct rare subtype that is genuinely monocular but associated with migrainous headache.

confusedWithConditions.name: Optic Neuritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Optic neuritis produces progressive monocular visual loss over hours to days, typically accompanied by pain on eye movement, and is associated with multiple sclerosis. Amaurosis fugax is sudden (seconds) and completely reversible within minutes, and is not associated with eye movement pain.

relatedConditions.name: Stroke
relatedConditions.relevance: Amaurosis fugax is a retinal TIA — the most powerful predictor of subsequent ischaemic stroke in the ipsilateral carotid territory, sharing the same embolic mechanisms.

relatedConditions.name: Carotid Artery Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: The hemispheric equivalent of amaurosis fugax — transient motor or speech TIA from the same carotid plaque; both conditions require the same urgent vascular workup and treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: An important differential diagnosis for any monocular vision loss; tonometry and optic disc assessment distinguish vascular from pressure-related visual disturbance.

relatedConditions.name: Central Pain Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Can develop after thalamic or cortical infarction in patients who progress from TIA to stroke in the visual or sensory pathways.

relatedConditions.name: Acoustic Neuroma
relatedConditions.relevance: Cerebellopontine angle mass occasionally considered when transient visual symptoms occur alongside ipsilateral cranial nerve findings; MRI readily excludes.

faqs.question: What is amaurosis fugax?
faqs.answer: Amaurosis fugax is a sudden, temporary, painless loss of vision in one eye lasting seconds to minutes, caused by brief blockage of the retinal blood supply. It is a type of transient ischaemic attack (TIA) and is treated as a medical emergency because it signals a high risk of stroke within the following hours and days.

faqs.question: What does amaurosis fugax feel like?
faqs.answer: Most patients describe a grey or black curtain descending over one eye from the top or sides, or a complete blackout of vision in one eye. The episode is painless, lasts 1–15 minutes, and vision returns to normal completely. Some describe looking through frosted glass or a brief dimming rather than complete blackness.

faqs.question: Is amaurosis fugax dangerous?
faqs.answer: Yes. Amaurosis fugax carries a 10–15% risk of stroke within 90 days, with half of those strokes occurring within the first 48 hours. It should be treated as a vascular emergency with same-day evaluation, immediate antiplatelet therapy, and urgent carotid and cardiac investigations. Early treatment reduces subsequent stroke risk by over 50%.

faqs.question: What causes amaurosis fugax?
faqs.answer: The most common cause is an embolus (small blood clot or cholesterol crystal) from atherosclerotic plaque in the ipsilateral carotid artery, which temporarily blocks the retinal artery. Atrial fibrillation, giant cell arteritis, polycythaemia, and retinal vasospasm from migraine are other important causes.

faqs.question: How is amaurosis fugax diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is based on the clinical history of sudden, painless, monocular vision loss lasting under 60 minutes. Carotid duplex ultrasound is the mandatory first investigation. Cardiac monitoring, echocardiography, and ophthalmological examination complete the workup. Blood tests include ESR to exclude giant cell arteritis in patients over 50.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for amaurosis fugax?
faqs.answer: Immediate aspirin 300 mg, followed by long-term antiplatelet therapy, high-intensity statin, and blood pressure control. Carotid endarterectomy is recommended within 2 weeks when carotid stenosis exceeds 70%. AF is treated with anticoagulation. Giant cell arteritis requires immediate high-dose prednisolone.

faqs.question: Will I lose my sight permanently from amaurosis fugax?
faqs.answer: In typical embolic amaurosis fugax, vision returns to completely normal within 30 minutes and permanent sight loss is very rare (under 1%). The main danger is subsequent stroke, not permanent blindness. Exception: giant cell arteritis causing amaurosis fugax can lead to permanent monocular blindness within hours without immediate steroid treatment.

faqs.question: What is the curtain sign in amaurosis fugax?
faqs.answer: The curtain sign describes the sensation of a grey or black shade descending from above (or rising from below) across one eye. It occurs because the retina fills with blood from top to bottom due to gravity, and when flow is restored, the curtain effect reverses. It is a classic but not universal description — fewer than 40% of patients report it.

faqs.question: How is amaurosis fugax different from a stroke?
faqs.answer: Amaurosis fugax resolves completely within 60 minutes and is monocular. Ischaemic stroke causes permanent or incomplete recovery, may affect the visual cortex (bilateral), and shows a corresponding infarct on MRI. Amaurosis fugax is a warning sign of stroke rather than a stroke itself.

faqs.question: Do I need carotid surgery for amaurosis fugax?
faqs.answer: Carotid endarterectomy is recommended when carotid duplex ultrasound shows 70–99% stenosis in the artery on the same side as the affected eye. Surgery within 2 weeks reduces 5-year ipsilateral stroke risk from 26% to 9%. Patients with less than 50% stenosis are managed with medications alone.

faqs.question: Can amaurosis fugax be caused by migraine?
faqs.answer: Yes, but rarely. Retinal migraine causes transient monocular visual loss from retinal artery vasospasm, usually in young adults without vascular risk factors, accompanied by or followed by unilateral headache. Unlike embolic amaurosis fugax, retinal migraine often involves positive visual phenomena (sparkling, zig-zag lights) before the blackout.

faqs.question: What is a Hollenhorst plaque?
faqs.answer: A Hollenhorst plaque is a bright yellow-orange cholesterol crystal visible at an arterial bifurcation on fundoscopy, derived from atherosclerotic plaque in the carotid artery. Its presence confirms upstream carotid atherosclerosis as the source of retinal emboli. It is seen in approximately 20–25% of patients after an embolic amaurosis fugax episode.

faqs.question: Can I drive after amaurosis fugax?
faqs.answer: No — UK DVLA regulations prohibit driving for at least 1 month after TIA including amaurosis fugax, until reviewed by a physician. In the US, regulations vary by state. Group 2 (HGV, bus) licences require a 3-month restriction. Clinicians must advise patients to stop driving and report the event to the licensing authority.

faqs.question: What causes amaurosis fugax in young people?
faqs.answer: In young adults, amaurosis fugax is more likely caused by cardioembolism (patent foramen ovale, AF, mitral valve prolapse), antiphospholipid syndrome, retinal migraine, cocaine use (vasospasm), or thrombophilia — rather than carotid atherosclerosis, which is uncommon before age 40. A full thrombophilia screen and echocardiogram are essential.

faqs.question: Is amaurosis fugax related to giant cell arteritis?
faqs.answer: Yes, and this is the most urgent cause. Giant cell arteritis (temporal arteritis) in patients over 50 can cause amaurosis fugax before permanent anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy and blindness. Warning signs include jaw pain on chewing, scalp tenderness, temporal headache, and ESR above 50 mm/hr. Immediate high-dose steroids prevent irreversible vision loss.
